---
title: "Sector-level methane emission estimates using CEH sectoral scaling"
date: 2026-06-24
author:
  - name: "Alice Ramsden"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
  - name: "Alison Redington"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
  - name: "Alistair Manning"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
categories:
  - methane
  - inverse-modelling
  - emissions-sectors
  - agriculture
  - energy
  - waste
image: /research-highlights/figures/methane-sector-ceh-scaling-fig1.png
description: >
  GEMMA has applied the InTEM atmospheric inverse modelling system to estimate
  UK methane emissions by sector — agriculture, energy, and waste — by combining
  top-down inversion output with spatial data from the Centre for Ecology &
  Hydrology (CEH) and EDGAR. Results reveal differences in magnitude and trend
  compared with the UK greenhouse gas inventory, with implications for how
  emissions are reported and tracked over time.
---

::: {.pdf-download-bar}
**Download this page as a PDF policy brief:**
[Download PDF](methane-sector-ceh-scaling.pdf){.btn .btn-sm .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.highlight-meta}
**Authors:** Alice Ramsden, Alison Redington, and Alistair Manning (Met Office) \
**Published:** 24 June 2026 \
**Related publication:** [Atmospheric observations consistent with reported decline in the UK's methane emissions (2013–2020)](https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-21-16257-2021)
:::

## Why this matters

The UK's official greenhouse gas inventory (GHGI) relies on "bottom-up"
calculations based on activity data and emission factors. Independently verifying
these figures at sector level — agriculture, energy, and waste — is important for
policy credibility and for identifying where reported trends may need revision.

GEMMA uses "top-down" atmospheric inverse modelling, which works backwards from
atmospheric measurements to estimate where and how much methane (CH₄) was emitted.
By combining these estimates with spatial information about where different sectors
operate — drawn from the Centre for Ecology & Hydrology (CEH) and the Emissions
Database for Global Atmospheric Research (EDGAR) — it becomes possible to attribute
modelled emissions to individual sectors.

This method is useful for comparing broad trends in sector-level emissions, though
a current lack of uncertainty data from CEH and EDGAR limits how precisely
differences with the inventory can be interpreted.

## What GEMMA did

GEMMA used the Inverse Technique for Emission Modelling (InTEM) to produce
estimates of total UK CH₄ emissions and applied CEH sectoral proportions to
split these into contributions from agriculture, energy, and waste. Results
were compared with the UK greenhouse gas inventory submitted in 2026.

Key findings by sector:

- **Agriculture:** InTEM estimates are slightly higher than the GHGI for most
  years and show no clear long-term trend.
- **Energy:** InTEM estimates lower emissions in 1990 than the GHGI, and the
  reduction between 1990 and 2025 is smaller in InTEM than in the inventory.
- **Waste:** InTEM estimates are lower than the GHGI for most years. Both
  approaches show a declining trend, but InTEM does not reproduce the peak in
  waste emissions in the mid-1990s reported in the inventory.

![InTEM sector-level CH₄ emission estimates compared with the UK greenhouse gas inventory, 1990–2025.](/research-highlights/figures/methane-sector-ceh-scaling-fig1.png){fig-alt="Two-panel figure showing InTEM CH4 emissions scaled by CEH sectoral proportions on the left, and UK greenhouse gas inventory CH4 estimates on the right, covering 1990 to 2025."}

## Scientific and policy impact

This work provides an independent check on the UK's sector-level methane reporting
and highlights specific areas — notably energy and waste — where top-down estimates
diverge from the inventory. These divergences do not necessarily indicate error in
either approach, but they flag areas warranting closer scrutiny and targeted
measurement.

The method is currently limited by the absence of formal uncertainty estimates from
CEH and EDGAR, which constrains how precisely differences can be interpreted. Addressing
this is a priority for future work.

## What comes next

- Develop a method for quantifying realistic uncertainty in these sector-level
  estimates, accounting for inversion posterior uncertainties and uncertainties in
  CEH/EDGAR sectoral attribution.
- Investigate the influence of point versus diffuse methane sources in CEH/EDGAR
  on the overall sector split; use high-resolution (6 km) CEH data to improve
  accuracy.
- Use spatially flat emission priors in the inversions to reduce the influence of
  the prior on the sector split.
- Extend the approach to other greenhouse gases, such as nitrous oxide (N₂O), to
  attribute seasonal patterns to specific sectors or geographical regions.
